# The Future of Vertical Farming: Transforming Global Food Production

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/1701277160694-E19D7VJEUF3XV3G0VH4Q/eden-green-vertical-produce+%281%29.jpg)

The future of vertical farming is here—and it's changing the way we grow our food. The world faces big challenges like crowded cities, rising temperatures, shrinking farmland, and limited water. Vertical farming offers a powerful solution.

Instead of planting crops across acres of land, vertical farming stacks plants in tall layers indoors, often inside cities. This method uses advanced technology to grow more food using fewer resources. It reduces the need for long-distance shipping, reduces water waste, and grows food year-round, no matter the weather outside.

## Why Vertical Farming Matters More than Ever

As global resources become more strained, the need for smarter farming methods has never been greater. Vertical farming addresses some of the most urgent challenges in agriculture: land scarcity and water shortages.

Let’s break down how vertical growing is changing the game.

### Addressing Land and Water Constraints

One of the biggest advantages of vertical growing is how little space it needs. By stacking plants in vertical layers, these farms can produce up to [390 times more](https://www.deloitte.com/global/en/issues/resilience/food-supply-resilience-david-rosenberg.html) food per square foot than traditional farms. That means more food, even in cities or places with very little farmland.

Water use is another area where vertical farms shine. Traditional farms can use [200 to 400 liters of water](https://www.agronomyjournals.com/article/view/844/7-6-39#:~:text=For%20example%2C%20producing%201%20kg%20of%20tomatoes,and%20aeroponics%20uses%20just%20about%2020%20litres.) just to grow a single kilogram of tomatoes. But with aeroponics—a type of vertical farming—that number can drop to just 20 liters. That’s [up to 90% less water](https://www.greenhousemag.com/news/hydroponics-provides-major-water-savings/) used, all while recycling nutrients and avoiding runoff.

This efficiency is especially important in areas hit hard by drought or with limited arable land. By moving food production indoors, vertical farming makes it possible to grow fresh crops where traditional methods just don’t work anymore.

**Related Article:**
[Hydroponics vs. Traditional Farming: A Comprehensive Comparison](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/hydroponics-vs-traditional-farming)

Discover how hydroponic systems use less water and land, making them powerful alternatives to soil-based farming.

### Meeting the Demands of Urbanization and Climate Change

More people are moving into cities than ever before. In China alone, the urban population is expected to reach [920 million](https://www.bonafideresearch.com/product/6207297104/china-vertical-farming-market). As cities grow, so does the pressure to feed people in crowded, resource-limited areas.

Vertical farming meets this challenge head-on. These systems are built to grow food right where people live—in or near cities. That means fewer trucks hauling produce nationwide, shorter travel times, and fresher food on shelves.

Even better, vertical farms don’t rely on sunny skies or perfect weather. Their climate-agnostic design makes them resilient to storms, heat waves, and droughts. Vertical farming reduces the risk of climate-related disruptions in the food supply by growing food indoors, year-round.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/1e39dd35-040b-46ab-8418-2a09a5923b8c/hydroponic+romaine+in+a+vertical+greenhouse+-+vertical+farming+at+eden+green+technology.jpg)

## Market Growth: A Rapidly Expanding Industry

Vertical farming is no longer a niche idea, but a booming business. Rising demand for fresh, local food and increased investment in ag-tech puts the industry in a position to grow fast.

Let’s look at the numbers and what’s fueling this rapid expansion.

### Global Projections Through 2035

The worldwide vertical farming market was valued at USD 3.76 billion in 2021. By 2030, it’s expected to reach [USD 26.37 billion](https://www.sphericalinsights.com/reports/vertical-farming-market), growing at a compound annual growth rate [(CAGR) of 24.42%](https://www.sphericalinsights.com/reports/vertical-farming-market). Even more impressively, some forecasts predict the market could hit [USD 96.33 billion by 2035](https://www.sphericalinsights.com/reports/vertical-farming-market).

So, what’s driving all this growth?

Three key factors:

**Urbanization**, which is creating more demand for local food;

**Climate change**, which is making outdoor farming less predictable; and

The push for **sustainable, space-efficient systems** that can operate in cities, deserts, or even underground.

Vertical farming’s strength lies in its adaptability. It doesn’t need fertile soil or perfect weather—just space, light, and smart systems. With the ability to grow crops year-round in controlled environments, it’s becoming the go-to option for feeding a growing, urban world.

### Regional Highlights: U.S., China, and Beyond

The global push toward vertical farming looks different from one region to the next. The drivers are similar—land pressure, climate shifts, and food demand. Yet, how countries respond depends on local needs and resources.

In the [United States](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/us-vertical-farming-market-report), the vertical farming market is projected to reach USD 2,453.2 million by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 19.1%. Much of this growth is tied to increased investment in Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA) and a strong push toward sustainable, tech-driven food systems.

[China](https://www.bonafideresearch.com/product/6207297104/china-vertical-farming-market), on the other hand, is scaling even faster. From 2024 to 2029, its vertical farming market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 27.91%. China has limited arable land, a rapidly growing urban population, and heavy government support for ag-tech parks and innovation subsidies. The country is using vertical farming to strengthen food security and reduce import reliance.

These regional trends show how vertical farming adapts to local pressures. In Asia, it’s often about solving land and population challenges. In North America, the focus leans toward climate resilience and sustainability through innovation.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/1688401707399-L1Y2K08X0SJR2FJBLQGA/grow-lights-up-close-produce.jpg)

## Technology Powering the Future of Vertical Farming

Vertical farming relies on smart, efficient technology to grow food in new ways. Advances in lighting, automation, and system design are helping farmers produce more while using fewer resources.

Let’s look at how these tools shape the next generation of indoor agriculture.

**Related Article:**
[What Is Vertical Farming?](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/what-is-vertical-farming)

Learn how vertical farming uses modern tools—from LED lighting to hydroponic systems—to grow food in stacked layers, close to the people who eat it.

### Automation, AI, and Smart Farming

One of the biggest shifts in vertical farming is the rise of automation. Today, robotics handle everything from planting and watering to harvesting and adjusting light levels. This cuts down on labor needs and keeps operations running smoothly around the clock.

AI systems are also transforming farm management. These smart tools monitor key variables—like nutrients, humidity, water, and lighting—and make automatic adjustments in real time. This ensures crops always have what they need to grow at their best.

Automation and AI make vertical farming more consistent and easier to scale by reducing human error and labor costs. Whether it’s one farm or a dozen, each one can follow the same optimized process for reliable, high-quality yields.

### The Rise of Data-Driven Agriculture

Behind the scenes, sensor data and analytics platforms are giving farmers more control than ever. These tools collect detailed feedback on how crops are growing—tracking everything from leaf color to moisture levels.

Farmers use this real-time data to fine-tune each crop's lighting schedules, nutrient mixes, and water use. The result? Less waste, better plant health, and higher yields. This smarter use of resources directly boosts return on investment.

Looking ahead, machine learning will allow farms to predict what crops need before problems arise. These systems will automatically fine-tune conditions based on growth stage and plant type. They can help farms become more efficient and productive.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/4e8caf34-b771-4d0c-a090-1bc5c06af88a/eden+green+vertical+greenhouse+-+hydroponic+basil.png)

## Vertical Farming as a Sustainability Solution

Sustainability is one of the biggest reasons vertical farming is gaining traction. With the climate under stress and natural resources growing scarce, this farming method offers an alternative. It's a way to grow food that’s both efficient and responsible.

This section will explore how vertical farming conserves resources, cuts emissions, and supports smarter, cleaner food systems.

**Related Article:**
[Vertical Indoor Farming: A Blueprint for Socially Responsible Businesses](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/vertical-farming-socially-responsible-businesses)

See how vertical farming supports sustainable business practices by blending food production with environmental care and community support.

### Maximizing Resource Efficiency

Vertical farms are designed to do more with less. By recirculating nutrient-rich water instead of letting it run off, they can use up to 90% less water than traditional farms. This closed-loop system saves water and ensures plants get exactly what they need.

Regarding yield, some vertical farms produce up to 390 times more food per square foot than field farms. That’s thanks to stacked growing systems, round-the-clock operation, and careful control of growing conditions.

New technologies also improve space use, water recovery, and nutrient precision. These innovations help reduce the inputs needed while getting more food out of every square foot.

Researchers and growers are also working on reducing waste, using AI and smart harvesting systems to pick only what’s needed and send it out through leaner, more direct supply chains.

**Related Article:**
[Vertical Farming Crop Yield per Acre vs. Traditional Farming](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/vertical-farming-crop-yield-per-acre)

Explore how vertical farms are breaking yield records by using less space and smarter growing systems to feed more people with fewer resources.

### Cutting Emissions Through Localized Production

Vertical farms help cut pollution by growing food close to where people live. This means fruits and vegetables don’t need to travel thousands of miles by truck or plane before reaching a store.

Most of farming's emissions come from moving food around. By skipping the long supply chain, vertical farms help reduce a major source of greenhouse gases.

These farms also fit right into cities. Many are built in unused buildings or on rooftops, which avoids the need to clear land or use heavy farm equipment. Plus, because they grow food indoors, they can run all year, no matter the weather. That strengthens our food system and reduces the risks of climate change.

### Renewable Energy Integration and Innovation

While vertical farms save water and land, they still face a big challenge: energy use. On average, it takes about [38.8 kilowatt-hours](https://www.agritecture.com/blog/2022/6/10/vertical-farms-have-the-vision-but-do-they-have-the-energy) (kWh) of electricity to grow just one kilogram of produce indoors.

To solve this, many farms are turning to renewable energy. Solar panels on rooftops and wind and geothermal systems are starting to power more farms. These clean sources help reduce the environmental cost of indoor growing.

Eden Green’s model offers an example of these benefits. Using natural sunlight and artificial light, their system uses [90% less light energy](https://www.edengreen.com/blog-collection/vertical-farming-socially-responsible-businesses) than vertical farms that rely only on LEDs. This hybrid approach of blending natural and electric light will likely become the standard for future farms.

These upgrades will make vertical farming more energy-efficient and better for the planet.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/ce4eeba3-26c7-47c9-b3fe-e0cd54c15403/eden-green-indoor-farming-infographic.png)

## Challenges on the Road to Scale

Vertical farming has huge potential—but scaling up isn’t easy. The two biggest barriers are high energy use and the cost of setting up and running these high-tech farms.

The good news is that the industry is already finding ways to tackle these challenges. From energy-efficient lighting and renewable energy to modular designs that cut costs, vertical farming is getting smarter and more affordable.

Still, practical, tech-enabled strategies are essential to make vertical farming widely available and sustainable in the long term. Here's a closer look at the main challenges and how innovators are working to solve them.

| Challenge | Solution |
| --- | --- |
| High energy consumption | Solar, wind, and hybrid lighting systems to reduce electricity needs |
| High upfront costs | Modular farm designs and public-private funding partnerships |
| Cost of advanced equipment and systems | Streamlined, scalable tech platforms; leasing models for startups |
| Limited access to farming knowledge | Training programs and AI-driven farm management tools |
| Market education and consumer awareness | Branding, farm-to-store transparency, and community engagement |

### What are the main challenges faced by vertical farming startups?

Startups in vertical farming face extra hurdles beyond the usual industry-wide issues. The biggest one is capital expense, as setting up a facility requires a lot of money upfront. Many also struggle with access to proper technology, as well as finding people trained in both farming and tech.

Education is another gap. Investors, retailers, and consumers often don’t fully understand the model yet. That means startups have to work harder to explain the value of what they do—and why it matters.

The key to overcoming these startup-specific barriers? Smart partnerships, clear messaging, and tools that make the tech easier to use and more affordable to access.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/0d3f8f3c-d027-4cb3-b3bf-3d540af33ea8/singapore.jpg)

## Global Models Leading the Way

Vertical farming is gaining traction around the world. Some countries are leading the charge with bold, government-backed plans. These models show how vertical farming can support national food goals and thrive in urban environments.

Two strong examples are Singapore and China. In these nations, population density, land shortages, and food security are driving innovation.

### Singapore’s 30 by 30 Vision

Singapore has a clear goal: to grow 30% of its nutritional needs locally by the year 2030. Known as the ["30 by 30" initiative](https://borgenproject.org/vertical-farming-in-singapore/), this plan is driven by a need to reduce reliance on food imports, which currently supply over 90% of the country's food.

Because Singapore has very little farmland, vertical farming is a key part of its strategy. Companies like Sky Greens use stacked systems that produce up to 10 times more food per square meter than traditional farms.

Another example is ComCrop, a rooftop farm that uses [90% less water](https://borgenproject.org/vertical-farming-in-singapore/) than standard agriculture. These projects show how vertical farming can work even in crowded cities with limited natural resources.

### China’s Policy-Driven Acceleration

China is facing rapid urban growth, with its urban population expected to reach [920 million people](https://www.bonafideresearch.com/product/6207297104/china-vertical-farming-market). This puts pressure on food systems, especially in a country with limited arable land.

To address the issue, the Chinese government is heavily backing vertical farming. Through policy incentives, financial support, and the development of agricultural tech parks, China is making vertical farming part of its long-term food strategy.

Between 2024 and 2029, the country’s vertical farming market is expected to grow at a rate of[27.91%](https://www.bonafideresearch.com/product/6207297104/china-vertical-farming-market)—one of the fastest in the world. A major part of this push involves weaving vertical farms into city planning. The initiative will help solve both land use problems and the need for fresh, local food in urban areas.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/9751c09d-592b-4b7a-af8c-ebba2f60fa9a/Multiple+Hydroponic+Herbs+at+Eden+Green+Technology+-+Vertical+Farming.jpg)

## What’s Next for Vertical Farming?

As vertical farming continues to evolve, the focus is shifting beyond the basics. The next wave of growth will center on expanding crop options, integrating farms into city architecture, and merging multiple technologies into seamless systems.

These changes will define the future of vertical farming and help it meet global food needs more effectively than ever before.

### Crop Diversification Beyond Leafy Greens

Until recently, vertical farms mostly focused on growing leafy greens like lettuce and kale. But that’s starting to change. Now, more farms are expanding into crops like tomatoes, herbs, bell peppers, strawberries, and cucumbers.

Tomatoes alone make up [nearly 25%](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/us-vertical-farming-market-report) of the market's revenue, showing just how important crop diversity has become. With better lighting and more advanced nutrient delivery systems, farms are also starting to grow more complex crops like fruiting plants and even root vegetables.

This kind of diversification matters. It allows vertical farms to reach more customers, meet more dietary needs, and become more financially sustainable.

### Integrating Farming into Urban Architecture

Cities worldwide are starting to build vertical farms right into homes, offices, and public buildings. This “grow-where-you-live” model makes it easier for people to get fresh food without needing land or long transport routes.

In China, vertical farming is already part of urban planning. It helps the country grow more food locally while using less space. These farms can be added to rooftops, built inside old warehouses, or included in new apartment buildings.

This kind of setup supports stronger food systems. It reduces the need for farmland, cuts down delivery time, and connects people directly to where their food is grown.

#### *How does vertical farming impact local communities?*

Vertical farming brings fresh food closer to the people who eat it. By growing food inside cities, it helps reduce food deserts—areas with limited access to healthy produce. It also creates local jobs and cuts down on emissions from food transport. Community-based vertical farms can even support schools and nonprofits, turning agriculture into a tool for education and support.

### Technology Convergence: AI, IoT, and Biotech

The future of vertical farming will depend on how well new technologies work together. Farms are already using AI and Internet of Things (IoT) tools to adjust light, water, nutrients, and temperature in real time.

Machine learning is another tool making a big impact. It can study plant growth and predict what each crop will need next. This helps farmers avoid waste and grow more food with fewer resources.

Looking ahead, biotechnology may also play a role. By improving plant traits, biotech can help crops grow better in indoor systems and adapt to specific farm environments.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/63064607eb816a4d50027fd1/ef45b273-4c28-477b-a499-68a1ad263348/giant+collard+greens+vertical+farming+eden+greentechnology.jpg)

## A New Era of Resilient Food Systems

Vertical farming is reshaping how we think about food. It offers real solutions to today’s biggest agricultural challenges of urban growth, climate change, land shortages, and water scarcity. By combining smart design with cutting-edge tools, vertical farming creates a more stable, sustainable way to grow food close to where people live.

This method can produce far more food per square foot than traditional farms while using significantly less water. Countries like Singapore and China are proving that national strategies can make vertical farming a key part of food security. And with the help of automation, AI, and biotech, the next generation of farms will be even more productive and efficient.

If you're exploring ways to make food systems more resilient, now’s the time to get involved. Learn how vertical farming can fit into your business, share this vision with others, and keep an eye on new developments in ag-tech. Investing in these systems today means building a better, stronger food future.

Vertical agriculture’s potential implications for food system resilience: outcomes of focus groups in the Fraser Valley, British Columbia - Ecology & Society This study explores local food system stakeholders’ account of vertical agriculture’s potential contributions to food system resilience, and specifically to food system decentralization, increasing stakeholder decision-making autonomy and diversifying food systems. We note here and in the conclusion that vertical agriculture is but one strategy to bolster local food systems and, like all emerging technologies for food production, has tradeoffs (see Glaros et al. We shift to highlighting key opportunities and barriers that these stakeholders emphasized for vertical agriculture’s contribution to food system resilience, including deductively examined data as well as themes that emerged from focus group discussions.

Research

ABSTRACT
--------

Food systems are vulnerable to socioeconomic and environmental changes, and increasing food system resilience has emerged as a key public policy priority. Vertical agriculture is one potential strategy to contribute to food system resilience. To date, the literature exploring this technology has focused on consumer perceptions, with less examination of broader social-ecological system considerations or on-the-ground implications for vulnerable food system actors. This study explores local food system stakeholders’ account of vertical agriculture’s potential contributions to food system resilience, and specifically to food system decentralization, increasing stakeholder decision-making autonomy and diversifying food systems. The results of an exploratory focus group with 16 participants, including local government officials and members of community food organizations, are presented. Participants discussed how vertical agriculture can contribute to food system decentralization by operating across a variety of scales and in public institutions, although local land-use zoning barriers, competing land use priorities, and lack of community acceptance may hinder its widespread implementation. Further, vertical agriculture may contribute to food system diversification by increasing the amount of regionally available produce in the face of future supply disruptions. The potential implications of vertical agriculture for increasing decision-making autonomy will vary, depending on whether it is governed at more or less accessible scales and forms. It is recommended that further research consider diverse scenarios and engage with wider stakeholder and community groups for vertical agriculture’s planning and implementation, particularly in less explored land use contexts, such as in agricultural regions or in public institutions.

INTRODUCTION
------------

It is increasingly recognized that our food systems are vulnerable to social, economic, and environmental change (FAO et al. 2023). A complex variety of factors make food systems more or less vulnerable to disturbances or shocks (Ericksen 2008). At a local or regional level, individuals having few options or strategies to access food and there being a low diversity of regionally grown crops have historically increased food system vulnerability (Fraser 2007). At a global level, the food system is ever more tightly interconnected through water, energy, transportation, and political systems, thereby increasing the potential for “synchronous failure” where the impacts of regional shocks (e.g., severe weather events) propagate over time and space (Homer-Dixon et al. 2015). Other factors, such as mounting global oligopoly of food producers, distributors, and retailers, have similarly exacerbated the vulnerability of the food system (Clapp 2023). Intensive monoculture practices and concomitant declines in agricultural biodiversity have increased the susceptibility of crops to pests, weeds, and disease (Altieri et al. 2015). Major events, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and war in Ukraine, have highlighted these dynamics in stark ways, where cross-border closures and supply disruptions have made food access and distribution challenging, exacerbating the risk of hunger worldwide (Carey et al. 2021). Such concerns and the comprehensive effects of interacting food systems have spurred a large body of literature exploring ways to increase food system resilience. Developing resilience in food systems can therefore be a lever of change for addressing food insecurity and vulnerability (Townsend et al. 2021). Resilience is a measure of a system’s ability to adapt and transform in response to disturbance (Walker et al. 2004).

One such effort to build food system resilience is vertical agriculture. Vertical agriculture refers to a suite of tools and technologies for growing food “upward,” not “outward,” thereby sparing land for conservation (Despommier 2010). Often (but not exclusively) using hydroponic, aeroponic, or aquaponic nutrient circulation systems alongside stacked grow-beds, vertical agriculture is highly water-, nutrient-, and land-efficient (Kozai et al. 2019, Halgamuge et al. 2021). Vertical agriculture promises, from a food system resilience standpoint, to safeguard food production from the impacts of climate change, such as adverse weather or pests and disease (Henry 2020). Similarly, it promises to contribute to developing more decentralized food systems, growing fresh vegetables locally and year-round. As currently practiced, however, vertical agriculture is relatively niche (in scale), not widespread, and limited to a few crops of moderate nutritional value (Goodman and Minner 2019).

The social scientific literature exploring vertical agriculture has only recently emerged. According to one recent systematic literature review, the socioeconomic literature regarding controlled environment agriculture comprises less than 10% of the current body of research, leading authors to argue that “socio-economic research...still lags behind biological or technical research” (Dsouza et al. 2023:8). Our research contributes to addressing this gap. Further, social scientific literature has largely focused on consumer perceptions of vertical agriculture, and specifically if consumers are willing to buy or try vertical agriculture products. Research has found positive perceptions of vertical agriculture’s contribution to fresh local food availability and utilization of vacant or small spaces, as well as skepticism regarding its “naturalness,” health aspects, nutrition, and impact on conventional food systems (Specht et al. 2019, Broad et al. 2022).

To date, literature has yet to explore civil society perspectives for vertical agriculture’s potential contribution to food system resilience, specifically in the North American context. In particular, few studies (with some exceptions: see Carolan 2020), have explored vertical agriculture from a planning perspective, asking where vertical agriculture fits in local and regional food systems. Doing so can elucidate key gaps and concerns that civil society stakeholders have as vertical agriculture is being considered as a potentially feasible strategy to build food system resilience in cities or regions. It is crucial to gather public views of vertical agriculture and identify existential and perceived barriers or potential hindrances to achieving its most widespread benefits. In particular, it is important to understand the perspectives of members of civil society who are actively involved in governing and shaping local food systems, such as local government, food system organizations, farmers, and retailers. Doing so can help to identify tensions and synergies in terms of new food production technology implementation and their potential implications for food system resilience. We note here and in the conclusion that vertical agriculture is but one strategy to bolster local food systems and, like all emerging technologies for food production, has tradeoffs (see Glaros et al. 2022). Thus, we suggest that vertical agriculture should be considered among a variety of strategies and approaches to increase food system resilience (Newman et al. 2023).

This study is the first stage of a broader research portfolio, working with consumers, local government, industry, and planners to develop scenarios for the implementation of vertical agriculture in British Columbia. In this initial research, we sought to understand and unpack the prospects of vertical agriculture’s contribution to food system resilience. We focus on exploring various civil society stakeholder accounts from the Lower Mainland of British Columbia, Canada, as it is a highly agricultural region of Canada that continues to experience severe impacts of climate change. We begin by defining food system resilience and how we used it as a theoretical framework to analyze our data. We shift to highlighting key opportunities and barriers that these stakeholders emphasized for vertical agriculture’s contribution to food system resilience, including deductively examined data as well as themes that emerged from focus group discussions.

THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK
---------------------

Multiple definitions for and interpretations of resilience exist in the literature, and the term itself has evolved over the past several decades. Earlier definitions of resilience refer to a system’s ability to resist disturbance: to stay the same (Pimm 1984). Literature from the discipline of ecology instead defined resilience as a system’s capacity to maintain its function over time (Gunderson and Holling 2001). Notions of adaptation and transformation were subsequently introduced, recognizing the existence of system tipping points and the potential for reorganizing and designing new systems altogether (Walker et al. 2004). Resilience has also been applied to examine social systems, wherein it has been defined as the ability of a community or group to cope with external stresses (Adger 2000).

Resilience is often explored within the context of food systems, being defined as the “capacity over time of a food system and its units at multiple levels, to provide sufficient, appropriate and accessible food to all, in the face of various and even unforeseen disturbances” (Tendall et al. 2015:18). However, specific metrics for or definitions of food system resilience are ill defined, particularly regarding the following questions: resilience of what, to what? Resilience for whom? Resilience for how long? (Cretney 2014, Meerow and Newell 2019, Zurek et al. 2022). For the purpose of this study, we view a resilient food system as one that maintains the systems’ wide preconditions so as to ensure food security in response to social, economic, and environmental uncertainties, similar to Hodbod and Eakin (2015).

Several factors reduce food system resilience, including lack of on-farm diversity, exposure and vulnerability to disturbances through hyper-connectivity, and lack of farmer decision-making autonomy (Rotz and Fraser 2015). Studies also define targeted areas for building food system resilience and link sustainability dimensions directly to resilience considerations, such as those that recognize that social factors affect a person’s or community’s capacity to respond to food insecurity (Kaiser 2019). Other examples include research that indicates intensive monocultures provide little ecological value (Onaindia et al. 2013) while being extremely vulnerable to pests and disease, and literature that highlights how food import-dependency can result in economic vulnerability (Schipanski et al. 2015).

Recognizing these conceptual ambiguities, we distilled from the literature a few key principles for increasing food system resilience and used these to deductively shape our thematic analysis of focus group data (Hendrickson 2015, Rotz and Fraser 2015, van Wassanaer et al. 2021). These three principles are as follows:

1.   Decentralizing food production operations;
2.   Increasing the agency and capacity of actors throughout the supply chain to exercise choice, particularly for those most vulnerable (e.g., low-income groups, temporary agricultural workers); and
3.   Increasing diversity within the food system, especially across the following three areas: number of crop species grown, places in which crops are grown, and places where people can access food.

Below, we describe each of these principles in further detail, including literature relevant to food systems and food system resilience. We acknowledge that definitions of resilience are contested, especially as related to how to measure, indicate, or otherwise increase/decrease resilience. However, our purpose in this paper is not so much to define resilience per se as to better understand if and how vertical agriculture can contribute to this normative goal. Although others may or may not agree with our characterization of resilience, we nevertheless consider the following principles as key components of a potentially more comprehensive definition. We recognize that these principles are likely to have inherent tradeoffs and are not ubiquitous in all vertical farming contexts. Our case study, however, illustrates how we captured some of the critical aspects of resilience relevant to vertical agriculture in the Fraser Valley region of British Columbia. As a result, the principles guided our exploration and evaluation of vertical agriculture benefits as well as adoption barriers in a more practical manner.

### Decentralization

Food systems have become increasingly concentrated and consolidated within the private sector (Clapp 2023). Concentration of food systems increases their opacity, where it becomes difficult to trace commodity flows, actor networks, or other important information across the global value chain (Clapp 2014). This has implications for consumer trust (knowing where and how food was produced), responding to disease and pest outbreaks, or assigning responsibility to malpractice and “rule breaking” as it occurs (Tian 2016, Iost Filho et al. 2019). Highly centralized, consolidated food systems are vulnerable because they have decreased capacity to adapt or transform in the face of disturbance (Hendrickson 2015). Decreasing concentration is an important food system development strategy because it helps to increase supply chain transparency and prevent widespread disruptions resulting from synchronous failure between tightly interconnected systems (Homer-Dixon et al. 2015). Decentralizing food systems would involve increasing the number of food system actors, from production through consumption and waste, operating at small and medium scales for local/regional distribution.

### Agency and choice

Where actors within food systems have fewer opportunities to make decisions and choices (i.e., exercise agency) in response to changes or disturbances, a system can be said to lack resilience. This is acute for small and medium scales of farmers, where external factors, such as sectoral concentration (Rotz and Fraser 2015) and climate change (Higgins et al. 2017), can disincentivize the adoption of new tools, crops, technologies, production practices, or regulatory requirements. Farmers having the ability to respond to disturbance, both as an adaptation and/or as a way of transformation in the face of disturbance, is crucial from a food system resilience lens. These challenges are also acute for consumers, in particular for vulnerable members of the public who may lack food security or the knowledge and resources for accessing food in the wake of adversities. Having diverse options to procure food (from the market, shared between neighbors, grown at home, etc.) is important to maintain individual food security (Sen 1983, Fraser 2007). Agency and choice are also imperative for small-scale producers specializing in local distribution. These producers often are in tune with consumer preferences but are at a competitive disadvantage compared to large-scale businesses (producers-retailers-distributors) better positioned (by virtue of economies of scale) to influence food policy decisions. When a small number of large-scale producers have the most power to affect change in the food system, opportunities for small and medium-scale producers to advocate for themselves and respond to disturbance is reduced (Rotz and Fraser 2015).

### Diversity

Systems can be described as more resilient if they have a high level of diversity and what some term functional redundancy: a concept from ecology for when more than one component of a system performs a similar role. As one example, if there are many producers growing diverse crops, a food system might be considered to have high functional redundancy, compared to one where a single producer is growing one or two crops. Food systems are more susceptible to pests, disease, and other disturbances that can easily transmit throughout a system when there is less crop and genetic diversity (Rotz and Fraser 2015). Diversity also translates to socioeconomic factors, including the function of food systems. For example, Hodbod and Eakin (2015) find that singular prioritization of a food system function (e.g., profit maximization) can precipitate vulnerabilities. Hertel et al. (2021) state that any attempt to integrate diversification of food production will need to transcend soil-based farming and encompass other subsectors, such as vertical and urban farming.

METHODS
-------

This research was conducted in collaboration with three industry partners: QuantoTech Solutions, i-Open Technologies, and Direct Food Store. QuantoTech Solutions develops and operates vertical farms; i-Open Technologies Group designed and developed _Agrilyze_, a map-based platform for storing, displaying, and analyzing food systems-related data; and Direct Food Store is an e-commerce and food distribution platform. The collaboration with industry partners was based on efforts to model and illustrate the potential benefits and drawbacks of developing the vertical agriculture industry in the Fraser Valley region, British Columbia (Fig. 1). This region hosts a strong and diverse agricultural sector, comprising a significant portion of the overall agricultural activity (39% of gross farm receipts in 2015) in British Columbia (Fraser Valley Regional District 2017). Further, the region was subject to major flooding events in November 2021, making this region an important case study in the context of integrated planning for resilience to climate change (Newell et al. 2022).

### Focus groups

Focus groups are a widely accepted and undertaken research activity to engage stakeholders and solicit their opinion on a given research topic (Nyumba et al. 2018). The protocol for this method typically involves a researcher delivering a set of semi-structured questions to a group of individuals (usually around 10), discussing the questions as a group, and qualitatively analyzing the resulting transcripts for themes. Prior to the focus group, researchers attended public virtual meetings with community food networks and organizations in the region. Members of these networks were invited to the focus group discussion, given their active and on-going practical planning, engagement, and program implementation experience related to food systems in the region of study. The researchers sent invitation letters to individuals within the community network and organization members as well as via these networks and organizations. The invitation was also sent to community members and food systems professionals/stakeholders with whom the researchers had previously engaged in other research efforts.

In total, 16 participants ultimately attended the focus group (Table 1). Research has found an optimal size for focus groups to be between seven and 10 individuals to ensure all voices have a better chance of being heard. As such, three breakout groups were created in order to undertake discussions in a more efficient and effective manner. Overall, the study participants represented a broad cross-section of community food system actors, including local governments, non-governmental organizations, industry stakeholders, and food retailers.

This focus group was exploratory and structured to gauge general themes and potential avenues for further research and study with stakeholders in the Fraser Valley region. The focus group ran for two hours in February 2022, and was conducted via Zoom and CoLabS, a web-based platform for interactive engagement and collaboration (see Jost et al. 2021 for similar use of this platform). Following full group discussion, two small groups (8 participants in each) were formed in Zoom breakout sessions. The focus group questions were categorized into three respective themes: “vertical farms and communities” (i.e., opportunities and barriers for vertical farm integration into local communities); “vertical farms and products/crops” (i.e., perspectives on vertically grown products); and “vertical farms and consumers” (i.e., about willingness to try vertical agriculture products). Questions within each theme focused on the potential contribution of vertical agriculture to food system resilience and sustainability. The first few minutes of each session began with participants posting comments related to the questions of the theme in CoLabS. Researchers then facilitated discussion using these comments.

### Analysis

To deductively examine our qualitative data we adopted three following principles of food system resilience: how vertical agriculture development may decentralize food systems, increase individual agency and capacity to respond to disturbances, and diversify food systems. A combination of deductive analysis (using the three principles for food system resilience defined above) and inductive approaches (cataloguing additional and emergent themes outside the three principles) were used to thematically code the focus group data (see Tables 2 and 3). Combining deductive with inductive approaches to data analysis is a strategy to ensure that a holistic account of all that was said during the focus group is captured (Azungah 2018). enriched_points.expanded_content: Vertical farming integrates food production within urban areas, minimizing transportation emissions and providing fresh produce directly to consumers, thus enhancing urban food security.
By establishing farms in underserved areas, vertical farming combats food deserts, ensuring access to nutritious food for communities lacking fresh produce options.
Vertical farms create jobs and stimulate local economies by sourcing materials and labor locally, while reducing costs associated with long-distance food transportation.
